verses.text: A man from the house of Levi marries a Levite woman. She conceives and bears a son — and seeing he is a fine child, she hides him three months. The Hebrew calls him goodly, the same word used of light on the first day. Hope is being hidden against the river's edict.

A man from the house of Levi marries a Levite woman. She conceives and bears a son — and seeing he is a fine child, she hides him three months. The Hebrew calls him goodly, the same word used of light on the first day. Hope is being hidden against the river's edict.

When she can no longer hide him, she makes an ark of bulrushes, seals it with tar and pitch, places the baby inside, and sets it in the reeds at the river's brink. The Hebrew word for ark is the same one used for Noah's. His sister stands at a distance, watching to see what will happen.

When she can no longer hide him, she makes an ark of bulrushes, seals it with tar and pitch, places the baby inside, and sets it in the reeds at the river's brink. The Hebrew word for ark is the same one used for Noah's. His sister stands at a distance, watching to see what will happen.

Pharaoh's own daughter comes down to bathe at the river. She sees the basket, sends her maid to fetch it, and opens it — and the baby is crying. She has compassion: this is one of the Hebrew children. The very household that ordered his death now holds him gently.

Pharaoh's own daughter comes down to bathe at the river. She sees the basket, sends her maid to fetch it, and opens it — and the baby is crying. She has compassion: this is one of the Hebrew children. The very household that ordered his death now holds him gently.

Miriam steps forward: shall I find you a Hebrew woman to nurse him? Go, says the princess. Miriam fetches — her own mother. The baby will be nursed by the woman who bore him, paid by the woman who saved him. The whole rescue runs through women.

Miriam steps forward: shall I find you a Hebrew woman to nurse him? Go, says the princess. Miriam fetches — her own mother. The baby will be nursed by the woman who bore him, paid by the woman who saved him. The whole rescue runs through women.

Take this child away and nurse him for me; I will give you wages, says Pharaoh's daughter. The mother takes him home. Years later, the child returns to the palace. Pharaoh's daughter names him Moses — drawn out — because I drew him from the water. He grows up bilingual, bicultural, and quietly Hebrew.

Take this child away and nurse him for me; I will give you wages, says Pharaoh's daughter. The mother takes him home. Years later, the child returns to the palace. Pharaoh's daughter names him Moses — drawn out — because I drew him from the water. He grows up bilingual, bicultural, and quietly Hebrew.

When Moses is grown, he goes out to see his brethren — he chooses to know them. He sees an Egyptian beating a Hebrew. He looks this way and that, sees no one, kills the Egyptian, and buries him in the sand. His instinct is right; his method is wrong.

When Moses is grown, he goes out to see his brethren — he chooses to know them. He sees an Egyptian beating a Hebrew. He looks this way and that, sees no one, kills the Egyptian, and buries him in the sand. His instinct is right; his method is wrong.

The next day he sees two Hebrews fighting and asks the aggressor: why are you striking your neighbor? The man snaps: who made you a prince and a judge? Are you going to kill me as you killed the Egyptian? Moses is afraid: surely this thing is known. Forty more years before he is ready.

The next day he sees two Hebrews fighting and asks the aggressor: why are you striking your neighbor? The man snaps: who made you a prince and a judge? Are you going to kill me as you killed the Egyptian? Moses is afraid: surely this thing is known. Forty more years before he is ready.
Pharaoh hears and tries to kill him. Moses flees and settles in the land of Midian. He sits down by a well. Another biblical romance, another well — but Moses doesn't know it yet.

The priest of Midian has seven daughters. They come to water their father's flock, but the local shepherds drive them off. Moses stands up, defends them, and waters the flock. Out of his own land, he becomes the protector he failed to be at home.

The priest of Midian has seven daughters. They come to water their father's flock, but the local shepherds drive them off. Moses stands up, defends them, and waters the flock. Out of his own land, he becomes the protector he failed to be at home.

They come back to their father Reuel earlier than usual. How is it? he asks. An Egyptian delivered us from the shepherds and drew water for the flock. Reuel: where is he? Why did you leave him? Call him, that he may eat bread. Hospitality is the first language of the desert.

They come back to their father Reuel earlier than usual. How is it? he asks. An Egyptian delivered us from the shepherds and drew water for the flock. Reuel: where is he? Why did you leave him? Call him, that he may eat bread. Hospitality is the first language of the desert.

They come back to their father Reuel earlier than usual. How is it? he asks. An Egyptian delivered us from the shepherds and drew water for the flock. Reuel: where is he? Why did you leave him? Call him, that he may eat bread. Hospitality is the first language of the desert.

Moses settles with Reuel and marries his daughter Zipporah. She bears him a son, whom he names Gershom — meaning sojourner — for he said, I have been a stranger in a strange land. The name records his exile rather than his rescue.

Moses settles with Reuel and marries his daughter Zipporah. She bears him a son, whom he names Gershom — meaning sojourner — for he said, I have been a stranger in a strange land. The name records his exile rather than his rescue.

Time passes; the king of Egypt dies, but the bondage does not. The children of Israel groan and cry out from their slavery — and their cry rises up to God. God hears their groaning. God remembers His covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. God looks upon the children of Israel — and God knows. Four verbs, all God's, set everything in motion.

God remembers His covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. God looks upon the children of Israel — and God knows. Four verbs, all God's, set everything in motion.

Time passes; the king of Egypt dies, but the bondage does not. The children of Israel groan and cry out from their slavery — and their cry rises up to God. God hears their groaning. God remembers His covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. God looks upon the children of Israel — and God knows. Four verbs, all God's, set everything in motion.
